patient_history.chief_complaint: vaginal bleeding at 22 weeks gestation with expulsion of large pelvic mass
known_conditions: pregnancy (gravida 4 para 0, 22 weeks gestation at initial presentation)
posterior vaginal wall defect status post repair
high-grade vaginal sarcoma (ER/PR positive, diagnosed 3 months postpartum)

patient_transcript:
Doctor: Good morning. How can I help you today?
Patient: Good morning, doctor. I was referred here from another hospital. I'm 27 weeks pregnant and I had a very scary episode a few weeks ago.
Doctor: I'm glad you came in. Can you walk me through what happened?
Patient: At 22 weeks, I started having vaginal bleeding. I went to the emergency room and they admitted me for bedrest. About two days later, I got terrible pelvic pain and contractions, and I passed a large mass vaginally.
Doctor: That sounds very distressing. Were there any fetal parts in the mass?
Patient: No, the doctors said there were no fetal contents. The baby was fine — they checked the heart tones and everything was okay.
Doctor: Do you know what the pathology showed on that mass?
Patient: Yes, they told me it was a very aggressive-looking tumor under the microscope, with spindle-shaped cells and giant cells. The pathologist said it could be an endometrial stromal sarcoma or an undifferentiated uterine sarcoma, but they weren't sure.
Doctor: I see. And what happened after that — did they do any procedure at the outside hospital?
Patient: Yes, they took me to the operating room and found a hole in the back wall of my vagina that was bleeding. They stitched it closed. My cervix was closed and the baby was still okay.
Doctor: Good. Can you tell me about your obstetric history?
Patient: This is my fourth pregnancy. I've had two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage.
Doctor: Any prior gynecologic problems — abnormal pap smears, fibroids, infertility?
Patient: No, nothing like that. My periods were always regular, no pain with sex, no unusual bleeding before this pregnancy.
Doctor: Any family history of cancer?
Patient: Yes, actually. Two of my family members had cervical cancer and two had endometrial cancer.
Doctor: That's important to know. We'll order an MRI today to get a better picture of what's happening. Has the fetus had any issues noted on ultrasound?
Patient: Yes, one of the baby's kidneys looks abnormal — they said it's dysplastic.
Doctor: Okay. And how are you feeling now at 27 weeks?
Patient: I feel okay. No more bleeding, no pelvic pain. I'm just worried about what this mass was.
Doctor: Understood. We're going to coordinate with our gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine teams to make the best plan for you and your baby.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I just want to make sure we're both safe.
chart_notes: 31yo G4P0 African American woman at 22 weeks gestation presented to outside hospital with vaginal bleeding. Obstetric history: 2 therapeutic abortions, 1 spontaneous abortion. Fetal anomaly: unilateral multi-cystic dysplastic kidney noted on prenatal ultrasound. Family history positive for cervical cancer (x2) and endometrial cancer (x2). Admitted for bedrest; 48h later developed pelvic pain and uterine contractions and expelled an 11 × 9 × 5 cm mass vaginally with no fetal contents. Pathology: highly cellular tumor with spindle-shaped cells, bizarre multinucleated giant cells, focal myxomatous change, mitotic count >50/10 hpf; IHC positive for CD10, SMA, ER, PR, EMA; differential endometrial stromal sarcoma vs. undifferentiated uterine sarcoma. EUA at outside hospital: 3 × 3 cm posterior vaginal wall defect with active bleeding, sutured for hemostasis; cervix long, closed, high; fetal heart tones normal. Discharged stable. Referred at 27 weeks to MFM and gyn-onc. At our institution: no prior abnormal gyn history, no dyspareunia, no bulk symptoms, normal pre-pregnancy periods. Pap smear 27 weeks: normal, HPV negative. Pelvic exam: no residual vaginal mass. Ultrasound: no intrauterine myomas. MRI pelvis/abdomen/chest without contrast at 27 weeks: Nabothian cyst in cervix, multiloculated cystic fetal renal structure consistent with dysplastic kidney, anterior placenta; no metastatic disease. After multidisciplinary discussion, planned cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks. Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 doses (first dose at 35w6d). At 36w1d: cesarean hysterectomy, bilateral salpingectomy, peritoneal biopsies; delivered male infant 2520g, Apgars 9/9; normal pelvic organs; no lymphadenopathy; no metastatic disease. Neonate to NICU for prematurity; neonatal US confirmed right MCDK; discharged home day 2. Final surgical pathology: benign, no malignant or neoplastic cells. 6-week postpartum: normal pelvic exam. 3 months postpartum: 1 cm posterior vaginal wall lesion; biopsy showed high-grade sarcoma, ER/PR positive. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: no metastatic disease. Upper vaginectomy and proctoscopy: 1.5 cm polypoid posterior vaginal wall lesion 5 cm from apex; 1 cm circumferential margin obtained; pathology high-grade sarcoma with negative margins; morphologically similar to original outside hospital specimen; all other biopsies negative. Marker seeds placed. Adjuvant HDR vaginal brachytherapy: EQD2 45 Gy whole vagina, 60 Gy to post-op bed. CT at 36 months: no evidence of disease. Disease-free 58 months after brachytherapy.
